content.text:
Have you ever dropped something into water and watched it sink to the bottom — or float on top? There's a science reason for that!

🚢 Why do some things float?
An object floats when it is less dense than water. Dense means how tightly packed the material is. A wooden block has air spaces inside, making it less dense — it floats!

⚓ Why do some things sink?
An object sinks when it is denser than water. A rock is very densely packed with no air spaces — it sinks!

🛳️ Wait — how do ships float?
A giant steel ship seems like it should sink, but it doesn't! That's because the shape matters. A ship is hollow and filled with air inside. Its large shape pushes a lot of water out of the way — this is called BUOYANCY. The water pushes back up on the ship and keeps it floating.

Scientist Archimedes figured this out over 2,000 years ago! He said: an object in water is pushed up by a force equal to the weight of water it displaces (pushes aside).

🔍 Testing Sink or Float:
You can predict whether something will sink or float by feeling if it's heavy for its size (dense = sinks) or light for its size (less dense = floats).

Floating and sinking is an important part of physics that helps engineers build boats, submarines, and even life jackets!

diagrams.alt: Diagram showing objects that sink and objects that float in water

experiment.instructions: Drag objects into the water tank to test if they sink or float!

combinations.resultLabel: The rubber duck floats! It's less dense than water — the air inside helps it stay on top. The water pushes up on it with a force called buoyancy!

practiceQuestions.prompt: Why does a rock sink in water?

options.text: Because it is denser than water
Because it has air spaces inside

practiceQuestions.hint: Dense objects sink. A rock is tightly packed — how does its density compare to water?

options.text: The force of gravity pulling down
The upward push of water on an object

practiceQuestions.hint: Buoyancy is what keeps ships floating — it pushes... which direction?

practiceQuestions.prompt: Why does a hollow steel ship float even though steel is heavy?

options.text: Because its hollow shape traps air and displaces a lot of water
Because seawater is not real water

practiceQuestions.hint: Think about how the ship's shape is different from a solid block of steel.

questions.prompt: Which object would most likely FLOAT?
An object floats when it is:

options.text: Exactly the same size as water

questions.prompt: Who discovered the principle of buoyancy about 2,000 years ago?
What does 'displace' mean when talking about buoyancy?
What helps a life jacket keep a person floating?

options.text: It contains foam or air that makes the person less dense than water
It pushes down on the water
